3	require "rails/railtie"
4	require "rails/engine/railties"
5	require "active_support/callbacks"
6	require "active_support/core_ext/object/try"
7	require "pathname"
8	
9	module Rails
10	  # +Rails::Engine+ allows you to wrap a specific \Rails application or subset of
11	  # functionality and share it with other applications or within a larger packaged application.
12	  # Every Rails::Application is just an engine, which allows for simple
13	  # feature and application sharing.
14	  #
15	  # Any +Rails::Engine+ is also a Rails::Railtie, so the same
16	  # methods (like {rake_tasks}[rdoc-ref:Rails::Railtie::rake_tasks] and
17	  # {generators}[rdoc-ref:Rails::Railtie::generators]) and configuration
18	  # options that are available in railties can also be used in engines.
19	  #
20	  # == Creating an Engine
21	  #
22	  # If you want a gem to behave as an engine, you have to specify an +Engine+
23	  # for it somewhere inside your plugin's +lib+ folder (similar to how we
24	  # specify a +Railtie+):
25	  #
26	  #   # lib/my_engine.rb
27	  #   module MyEngine
28	  #     class Engine < Rails::Engine
29	  #     end
30	  #   end
31	  #
32	  # Then ensure that this file is loaded at the top of your <tt>config/application.rb</tt>
33	  # (or in your +Gemfile+), and it will automatically load models, controllers, and helpers
34	  # inside +app+, load routes at <tt>config/routes.rb</tt>, load locales at
35	  # <tt>config/locales/**/*</tt>, and load tasks at <tt>lib/tasks/**/*</tt>.
36	  #
37	  # == Configuration
38	  #
39	  # Like railties, engines can access a config object which contains configuration shared by
40	  # all railties and the application.
41	  # Additionally, each engine can access <tt>autoload_paths</tt>, <tt>eager_load_paths</tt> and
42	  # <tt>autoload_once_paths</tt> settings which are scoped to that engine.
43	  #
44	  #   class MyEngine < Rails::Engine
45	  #     # Add a load path for this specific Engine
46	  #     config.autoload_paths << File.expand_path("lib/some/path", __dir__)
47	  #
48	  #     initializer "my_engine.add_middleware" do |app|
49	  #       app.middleware.use MyEngine::Middleware
50	  #     end
51	  #   end
52	  #
53	  # == Generators
54	  #
55	  # You can set up generators for engines with <tt>config.generators</tt> method:
56	  #
57	  #   class MyEngine < Rails::Engine
58	  #     config.generators do |g|
59	  #       g.orm             :active_record
60	  #       g.template_engine :erb
61	  #       g.test_framework  :test_unit
62	  #     end
63	  #   end
64	  #
65	  # You can also set generators for an application by using <tt>config.app_generators</tt>:
66	  #
67	  #   class MyEngine < Rails::Engine
68	  #     # note that you can also pass block to app_generators in the same way you
69	  #     # can pass it to generators method
70	  #     config.app_generators.orm :datamapper
71	  #   end
72	  #
73	  # == Paths
74	  #
75	  # Applications and engines have flexible path configuration, meaning that you
76	  # are not required to place your controllers at <tt>app/controllers</tt>, but
77	  # in any place which you find convenient.
78	  #
79	  # For example, let's suppose you want to place your controllers in <tt>lib/controllers</tt>.
80	  # You can set that as an option:
81	  #
82	  #   class MyEngine < Rails::Engine
83	  #     paths["app/controllers"] = "lib/controllers"
84	  #   end
85	  #
86	  # You can also have your controllers loaded from both <tt>app/controllers</tt> and
87	  # <tt>lib/controllers</tt>:
88	  #
89	  #   class MyEngine < Rails::Engine
90	  #     paths["app/controllers"] << "lib/controllers"
91	  #   end
92	  #
93	  # The available paths in an engine are:
94	  #
95	  #   class MyEngine < Rails::Engine
96	  #     paths["app"]                 # => ["app"]
97	  #     paths["app/controllers"]     # => ["app/controllers"]
98	  #     paths["app/helpers"]         # => ["app/helpers"]
99	  #     paths["app/models"]          # => ["app/models"]
100	  #     paths["app/views"]           # => ["app/views"]
101	  #     paths["lib"]                 # => ["lib"]
102	  #     paths["lib/tasks"]           # => ["lib/tasks"]
103	  #     paths["config"]              # => ["config"]
104	  #     paths["config/initializers"] # => ["config/initializers"]
105	  #     paths["config/locales"]      # => ["config/locales"]
106	  #     paths["config/routes.rb"]    # => ["config/routes.rb"]
107	  #   end
108	  #
109	  # The <tt>Application</tt> class adds a couple more paths to this set. And as in your
110	  # <tt>Application</tt>, all folders under +app+ are automatically added to the load path.
111	  # If you have an <tt>app/services</tt> folder for example, it will be added by default.
112	  #
113	  # == Endpoint
114	  #
115	  # An engine can also be a Rack application. It can be useful if you have a Rack application that
116	  # you would like to provide with some of the +Engine+'s features.
117	  #
118	  # To do that, use the ::endpoint method:
119	  #
120	  #   module MyEngine
121	  #     class Engine < Rails::Engine
122	  #       endpoint MyRackApplication
123	  #     end
124	  #   end
125	  #
126	  # Now you can mount your engine in application's routes:
127	  #
128	  #   Rails.application.routes.draw do
129	  #     mount MyEngine::Engine => "/engine"
130	  #   end
131	  #
132	  # == Middleware stack
133	  #
134	  # As an engine can now be a Rack endpoint, it can also have a middleware
135	  # stack. The usage is exactly the same as in <tt>Application</tt>:
136	  #
137	  #   module MyEngine
138	  #     class Engine < Rails::Engine
139	  #       middleware.use SomeMiddleware
140	  #     end
141	  #   end
142	  #
143	  # == Routes
144	  #
145	  # If you don't specify an endpoint, routes will be used as the default
146	  # endpoint. You can use them just like you use an application's routes:
147	  #
148	  #   # ENGINE/config/routes.rb
149	  #   MyEngine::Engine.routes.draw do
150	  #     get "/" => "posts#index"

5	require "action_view"
6	require "action_controller/log_subscriber"
7	require "action_controller/structured_event_subscriber"
8	require "action_controller/metal/params_wrapper"
9	
10	module ActionController
11	  # # Action Controller Base
12	  #
13	  # Action Controllers are the core of a web request in Rails. They are made up of
14	  # one or more actions that are executed on request and then either it renders a
15	  # template or redirects to another action. An action is defined as a public
16	  # method on the controller, which will automatically be made accessible to the
17	  # web-server through Rails Routes.
18	  #
19	  # By default, only the ApplicationController in a Rails application inherits
20	  # from `ActionController::Base`. All other controllers inherit from
21	  # ApplicationController. This gives you one class to configure things such as
22	  # request forgery protection and filtering of sensitive request parameters.
23	  #
24	  # A sample controller could look like this:
25	  #
26	  #     class PostsController < ApplicationController
27	  #       def index
28	  #         @posts = Post.all
29	  #       end
30	  #
31	  #       def create
32	  #         @post = Post.create params[:post]
33	  #         redirect_to posts_path
34	  #       end
35	  #     end
36	  #
37	  # Actions, by default, render a template in the `app/views` directory
38	  # corresponding to the name of the controller and action after executing code in
39	  # the action. For example, the `index` action of the PostsController would
40	  # render the template `app/views/posts/index.html.erb` by default after
41	  # populating the `@posts` instance variable.
42	  #
43	  # Unlike index, the create action will not render a template. After performing
44	  # its main purpose (creating a new post), it initiates a redirect instead. This
45	  # redirect works by returning an external `302 Moved` HTTP response that takes
46	  # the user to the index action.
47	  #
48	  # These two methods represent the two basic action archetypes used in Action
49	  # Controllers: Get-and-show and do-and-redirect. Most actions are variations on
50	  # these themes.
51	  #
52	  # ## Requests
53	  #
54	  # For every request, the router determines the value of the `controller` and
55	  # `action` keys. These determine which controller and action are called. The
56	  # remaining request parameters, the session (if one is available), and the full
57	  # request with all the HTTP headers are made available to the action through
58	  # accessor methods. Then the action is performed.
59	  #
60	  # The full request object is available via the request accessor and is primarily
61	  # used to query for HTTP headers:
62	  #
63	  #     def server_ip
64	  #       location = request.env["REMOTE_ADDR"]
65	  #       render plain: "This server hosted at #{location}"
66	  #     end
67	  #
68	  # ## Parameters
69	  #
70	  # All request parameters, whether they come from a query string in the URL or
71	  # form data submitted through a POST request are available through the `params`
72	  # method which returns a hash. For example, an action that was performed through
73	  # `/posts?category=All&limit=5` will include `{ "category" => "All", "limit" =>
74	  # "5" }` in `params`.
75	  #
76	  # It's also possible to construct multi-dimensional parameter hashes by
77	  # specifying keys using brackets, such as:
78	  #
79	  #     <input type="text" name="post[name]" value="david">
80	  #     <input type="text" name="post[address]" value="hyacintvej">
81	  #
82	  # A request coming from a form holding these inputs will include `{ "post" => {
83	  # "name" => "david", "address" => "hyacintvej" } }`. If the address input had
84	  # been named `post[address][street]`, the `params` would have included `{ "post"
85	  # => { "address" => { "street" => "hyacintvej" } } }`. There's no limit to the
86	  # depth of the nesting.
87	  #
88	  # ## Sessions
89	  #
90	  # Sessions allow you to store objects in between requests. This is useful for
91	  # objects that are not yet ready to be persisted, such as a Signup object
92	  # constructed in a multi-paged process, or objects that don't change much and
93	  # are needed all the time, such as a User object for a system that requires
94	  # login. The session should not be used, however, as a cache for objects where
95	  # it's likely they could be changed unknowingly. It's usually too much work to
96	  # keep it all synchronized -- something databases already excel at.
97	  #
98	  # You can place objects in the session by using the `session` method, which
99	  # accesses a hash:
100	  #
101	  #     session[:person] = Person.authenticate(user_name, password)
102	  #
103	  # You can retrieve it again through the same hash:
104	  #
105	  #     "Hello #{session[:person]}"
106	  #
107	  # For removing objects from the session, you can either assign a single key to
108	  # `nil`:
109	  #
110	  #     # removes :person from session
111	  #     session[:person] = nil
112	  #
113	  # or you can remove the entire session with `reset_session`.
114	  #
115	  # By default, sessions are stored in an encrypted browser cookie (see
116	  # ActionDispatch::Session::CookieStore). Thus the user will not be able to read
117	  # or edit the session data. However, the user can keep a copy of the cookie even
118	  # after it has expired, so you should avoid storing sensitive information in
119	  # cookie-based sessions.
120	  #
121	  # ## Responses
122	  #
123	  # Each action results in a response, which holds the headers and document to be
124	  # sent to the user's browser. The actual response object is generated
125	  # automatically through the use of renders and redirects and requires no user
126	  # intervention.
127	  #
128	  # ## Renders
129	  #
130	  # Action Controller sends content to the user by using one of five rendering
131	  # methods. The most versatile and common is the rendering of a template.
132	  # Also included with \Rails is Action View, which enables rendering of ERB
133	  # templates. It's automatically configured. The controller passes objects to the
134	  # view by assigning instance variables:
135	  #
136	  #     def show
137	  #       @post = Post.find(params[:id])
138	  #     end
139	  #
140	  # Which are then automatically available to the view:
141	  #
142	  #     Title: <%= @post.title %>
143	  #
144	  # You don't have to rely on the automated rendering. For example, actions that
145	  # could result in the rendering of different templates will use the manual
146	  # rendering methods:
147	  #
148	  #     def search
149	  #       @results = Search.find(params[:query])
150	  #       case @results.count
151	  #         when 0 then render action: "no_results"
152	  #         when 1 then render action: "show"
153	  #         when 2..10 then render action: "show_many"
154	  #       end
155	  #     end
156	  #
157	  # Read more about writing ERB and Builder templates in ActionView::Base.
158	  #
159	  # ## Redirects
160	  #
161	  # Redirects are used to move from one action to another. For example, after a
162	  # `create` action, which stores a blog entry to the database, we might like to
163	  # show the user the new entry. Because we're following good DRY principles
164	  # (Don't Repeat Yourself), we're going to reuse (and redirect to) a `show`
165	  # action that we'll assume has already been created. The code might look like
166	  # this:
167	  #
168	  #     def create
169	  #       @entry = Entry.new(params[:entry])
170	  #       if @entry.save
171	  #         # The entry was saved correctly, redirect to show
172	  #         redirect_to action: 'show', id: @entry.id
173	  #       else
174	  #         # things didn't go so well, do something else
175	  #       end
176	  #     end
177	  #
178	  # In this case, after saving our new entry to the database, the user is
179	  # redirected to the `show` method, which is then executed. Note that this is an
180	  # external HTTP-level redirection which will cause the browser to make a second
181	  # request (a GET to the show action), and not some internal re-routing which
182	  # calls both "create" and then "show" within one request.
183	  #
184	  # Learn more about `redirect_to` and what options you have in
185	  # ActionController::Redirecting.
186	  #
187	  # ## Calling multiple redirects or renders
188	  #
189	  # An action may perform only a single render or a single redirect. Attempting to
190	  # do either again will result in a DoubleRenderError:
191	  #
192	  #     def do_something
193	  #       redirect_to action: "elsewhere"
194	  #       render action: "overthere" # raises DoubleRenderError
195	  #     end
196	  #
197	  # If you need to redirect on the condition of something, then be sure to add
198	  # "return" to halt execution.
199	  #
200	  #     def do_something
201	  #       if monkeys.nil?
202	  #         redirect_to(action: "elsewhere")
203	  #         return
204	  #       end
205	  #       render action: "overthere" # won't be called if monkeys is nil
206	  #     end
207	  #
208	  class Base < Metal
209	    abstract!
210	
211	    # Shortcut helper that returns all the modules included in
212	    # ActionController::Base except the ones passed as arguments:
213	    #
214	    #     class MyBaseController < ActionController::Metal
215	    #       ActionController::Base.without_modules(:ParamsWrapper, :Streaming).each do |left|
216	    #         include left
217	    #       end
218	    #     end
219	    #
220	    # This gives better control over what you want to exclude and makes it easier to
221	    # create a bare controller class, instead of listing the modules required
222	    # manually.
223	    def self.without_modules(*modules)
224	      modules = modules.map do |m|
225	        m.is_a?(Symbol) ? ActionController.const_get(m) : m
226	      end
227	
228	      MODULES - modules
229	    end

265	      Rescue,
266	      Instrumentation,
267	      ParamsWrapper
268	    ].freeze
269

271	    # :stopdoc:
272	    include AbstractController::Rendering
273	    include AbstractController::Translation
274	    include AbstractController::AssetPaths
275	    include Helpers
276	    include UrlFor
277	    include Redirecting

content.content:
150	  #     get "/" => "posts#index"
151	  #   end
152	  #
153	  # == Mount priority
154	  #
155	  # Note that now there can be more than one router in your application, and it's better to avoid
156	  # passing requests through many routers. Consider this situation:
157	  #
158	  #   Rails.application.routes.draw do
159	  #     mount MyEngine::Engine => "/blog"
160	  #     get "/blog/omg" => "main#omg"
161	  #   end
162	  #
163	  # +MyEngine+ is mounted at <tt>/blog</tt>, and <tt>/blog/omg</tt> points to application's
164	  # controller. In such a situation, requests to <tt>/blog/omg</tt> will go through +MyEngine+,
165	  # and if there is no such route in +Engine+'s routes, it will be dispatched to <tt>main#omg</tt>.
166	  # It's much better to swap that:
167	  #
168	  #   Rails.application.routes.draw do
169	  #     get "/blog/omg" => "main#omg"
170	  #     mount MyEngine::Engine => "/blog"
171	  #   end
172	  #
173	  # Now, +Engine+ will get only requests that were not handled by +Application+.
174	  #
175	  # == Engine name
176	  #
177	  # There are some places where an Engine's name is used:
178	  #
179	  # * routes: when you mount an Engine with <tt>mount(MyEngine::Engine => '/my_engine')</tt>,
180	  #   it's used as default <tt>:as</tt> option
181	  # * rake task for installing migrations <tt>my_engine:install:migrations</tt>
182	  #
183	  # Engine name is set by default based on class name. For +MyEngine::Engine+ it will be
184	  # <tt>my_engine_engine</tt>. You can change it manually using the <tt>engine_name</tt> method:
185	  #
186	  #   module MyEngine
187	  #     class Engine < Rails::Engine
188	  #       engine_name "my_engine"
189	  #     end
190	  #   end
191	  #
192	  # == Isolated Engine
193	  #
194	  # Normally when you create controllers, helpers, and models inside an engine, they are treated
195	  # as if they were created inside the application itself. This means that all helpers and
196	  # named routes from the application will be available to your engine's controllers as well.
197	  #
198	  # However, sometimes you want to isolate your engine from the application, especially if your engine
199	  # has its own router. To do that, you simply need to call ::isolate_namespace. This method requires
200	  # you to pass a module where all your controllers, helpers, and models should be nested to:
201	  #
202	  #   module MyEngine
203	  #     class Engine < Rails::Engine
204	  #       isolate_namespace MyEngine
205	  #     end
206	  #   end
207	  #
208	  # With such an engine, everything that is inside the +MyEngine+ module will be isolated from
209	  # the application.
210	  #
211	  # Consider this controller:
212	  #
213	  #   module MyEngine
214	  #     class FooController < ActionController::Base
215	  #     end
216	  #   end
217	  #
218	  # If the +MyEngine+ engine is marked as isolated, +FooController+ only has
219	  # access to helpers from +MyEngine+, and <tt>url_helpers</tt> from
220	  # <tt>MyEngine::Engine.routes</tt>.
221	  #
222	  # The next thing that changes in isolated engines is the behavior of routes.
223	  # Normally, when you namespace your controllers, you also need to namespace
224	  # the related routes. With an isolated engine, the engine's namespace is
225	  # automatically applied, so you don't need to specify it explicitly in your
226	  # routes:
227	  #
228	  #   MyEngine::Engine.routes.draw do
229	  #     resources :articles
230	  #   end
231	  #
232	  # If +MyEngine+ is isolated, the routes above will point to
233	  # +MyEngine::ArticlesController+. You also don't need to use longer
234	  # URL helpers like +my_engine_articles_path+. Instead, you should simply use
235	  # +articles_path+, like you would do with your main application.
236	  #
237	  # To make this behavior consistent with other parts of the framework,
238	  # isolated engines also have an effect on ActiveModel::Naming. In a
239	  # normal \Rails app, when you use a namespaced model such as
240	  # +Namespace::Article+, ActiveModel::Naming will generate
241	  # names with the prefix "namespace". In an isolated engine, the prefix will
242	  # be omitted in URL helpers and form fields, for convenience.
243	  #
244	  #   polymorphic_url(MyEngine::Article.new)
245	  #   # => "articles_path" # not "my_engine_articles_path"
246	  #
247	  #   form_with(model: MyEngine::Article.new) do
248	  #     text_field :title # => <input type="text" name="article[title]" id="article_title" />
249	  #   end
250	  #
251	  # Additionally, an isolated engine will set its own name according to its
252	  # namespace, so <tt>MyEngine::Engine.engine_name</tt> will return
253	  # "my_engine". It will also set +MyEngine.table_name_prefix+ to "my_engine_",
254	  # meaning for example that +MyEngine::Article+ will use the
255	  # +my_engine_articles+ database table by default.
256	  #
257	  # == Using Engine's routes outside Engine
258	  #
259	  # Since you can now mount an engine inside application's routes, you do not have direct access to +Engine+'s
260	  # <tt>url_helpers</tt> inside +Application+. When you mount an engine in an application's routes, a special helper is
261	  # created to allow you to do that. Consider such a scenario:
262	  #
263	  #   # config/routes.rb
264	  #   Rails.application.routes.draw do
265	  #     mount MyEngine::Engine => "/my_engine", as: "my_engine"
266	  #     get "/foo" => "foo#index"
267	  #   end
268	  #
269	  # Now, you can use the <tt>my_engine</tt> helper inside your application:
270	  #
271	  #   class FooController < ApplicationController
272	  #     def index
273	  #       my_engine.root_url # => /my_engine/
274	  #     end
275	  #   end
276	  #
277	  # There is also a <tt>main_app</tt> helper that gives you access to application's routes inside Engine:
278	  #
279	  #   module MyEngine
280	  #     class BarController
281	  #       def index
282	  #         main_app.foo_path # => /foo
283	  #       end
284	  #     end
285	  #   end
286	  #
287	  # Note that the <tt>:as</tt> option given to mount takes the <tt>engine_name</tt> as default, so most of the time
288	  # you can simply omit it.
289	  #
290	  # Finally, if you want to generate a URL to an engine's route using
291	  # <tt>polymorphic_url</tt>, you also need to pass the engine helper. Let's
292	  # say that you want to create a form pointing to one of the engine's routes.
293	  # All you need to do is pass the helper as the first element in array with
294	  # attributes for URL:
295	  #
296	  #   form_with(model: [my_engine, @user])
297	  #
298	  # This code will use <tt>my_engine.user_path(@user)</tt> to generate the proper route.
299	  #
300	  # == Isolated engine's helpers
301	  #
302	  # Sometimes you may want to isolate an engine, but use helpers that are defined for it.
303	  # If you want to share just a few specific helpers you can add them to application's
304	  # helpers in ApplicationController:
305	  #
306	  #   class ApplicationController < ActionController::Base
307	  #     helper MyEngine::SharedEngineHelper
308	  #   end
309	  #
310	  # If you want to include all of the engine's helpers, you can use the #helper method on an engine's
311	  # instance:
312	  #
313	  #   class ApplicationController < ActionController::Base
314	  #     helper MyEngine::Engine.helpers
315	  #   end
316	  #
317	  # It will include all of the helpers from engine's directory. Take into account this does
318	  # not include helpers defined in controllers with helper_method or other similar solutions,
319	  # only helpers defined in the helpers directory will be included.
320	  #
321	  # == Migrations & seed data
322	  #
323	  # Engines can have their own migrations. The default path for migrations is exactly the same
324	  # as in application: <tt>db/migrate</tt>
325	  #
326	  # To use engine's migrations in application you can use the rake task below, which copies them to
327	  # application's dir:
328	  #
329	  #   $ rake ENGINE_NAME:install:migrations
330	  #
331	  # Note that some of the migrations may be skipped if a migration with the same name already exists
332	  # in application. In such a situation you must decide whether to leave that migration or rename the
333	  # migration in the application and rerun copying migrations.
334	  #
335	  # If your engine has migrations, you may also want to prepare data for the database in
336	  # the <tt>db/seeds.rb</tt> file. You can load that data using the <tt>load_seed</tt> method, e.g.
337	  #
338	  #   MyEngine::Engine.load_seed
339	  #
340	  # == Loading priority
341	  #
342	  # In order to change engine's priority you can use +config.railties_order+ in the main application.
343	  # It will affect the priority of loading views, helpers, assets, and all the other files
344	  # related to engine or application.
345	  #
346	  #   # load Blog::Engine with highest priority, followed by application and other railties
347	  #   config.railties_order = [Blog::Engine, :main_app, :all]
348	  class Engine < Railtie
349	    autoload :Configuration, "rails/engine/configuration"

5	require "active_support/core_ext/array/extract_options"
6	require "action_dispatch/middleware/stack"
7	
8	module ActionController
9	  # # Action Controller MiddlewareStack
10	  #
11	  # Extend ActionDispatch middleware stack to make it aware of options allowing
12	  # the following syntax in controllers:
13	  #
14	  #     class PostsController < ApplicationController
15	  #       use AuthenticationMiddleware, except: [:index, :show]
16	  #     end
17	  #
18	  class MiddlewareStack < ActionDispatch::MiddlewareStack # :nodoc:
19	    class Middleware < ActionDispatch::MiddlewareStack::Middleware # :nodoc:
20	      def initialize(klass, args, actions, strategy, block)
21	        @actions = actions
22	        @strategy = strategy
23	        super(klass, args, block)
24	      end
25	
26	      def valid?(action)
27	        @strategy.call @actions, action
28	      end
29	    end
30	
31	    def build(action, app = nil, &block)
32	      action = action.to_s
33	
34	      middlewares.reverse.inject(app || block) do |a, middleware|
35	        middleware.valid?(action) ? middleware.build(a) : a
36	      end
37	    end
38	
39	    private
40	      INCLUDE = ActiveSupport::Ractors.shareable_lambda(&->(list, action) { list.include? action })
41	      EXCLUDE = ActiveSupport::Ractors.shareable_lambda(&->(list, action) { !list.include? action })
42	      NULL    = ActiveSupport::Ractors.shareable_lambda(&->(list, action) { true })
43	
44	      def build_middleware(klass, args, block)
45	        options = args.extract_options!
46	        only   = Array(options.delete(:only)).map(&:to_s)
47	        except = Array(options.delete(:except)).map(&:to_s)
48	        args << options unless options.empty?
49	
50	        strategy = NULL
51	        list     = nil
52	
53	        if only.any?
54	          strategy = INCLUDE
55	          list     = only
56	        elsif except.any?
57	          strategy = EXCLUDE
58	          list     = except
59	        end
60	
61	        Middleware.new(klass, args, list, strategy, block)
62	      end
63	  end
64	
65	  # # Action Controller Metal
66	  #
67	  # `ActionController::Metal` is the simplest possible controller, providing a
68	  # valid Rack interface without the additional niceties provided by
69	  # ActionController::Base.
70	  #
71	  # A sample metal controller might look like this:
72	  #
73	  #     class HelloController < ActionController::Metal
74	  #       def index
75	  #         self.response_body = "Hello World!"
76	  #       end
77	  #     end
78	  #
79	  # And then to route requests to your metal controller, you would add something
80	  # like this to `config/routes.rb`:
81	  #
82	  #     get 'hello', to: HelloController.action(:index)
83	  #
84	  # The ::action method returns a valid Rack application for the Rails router to
85	  # dispatch to.
86	  #
87	  # ## Rendering Helpers
88	  #
89	  # By default, `ActionController::Metal` provides no utilities for rendering
90	  # views, partials, or other responses aside from some low-level setters such
91	  # as #response_body=, #content_type=, and #status=. To add the render helpers
92	  # you're used to having in a normal controller, you can do the following:
93	  #
94	  #     class HelloController < ActionController::Metal
95	  #       include AbstractController::Rendering
96	  #       include ActionView::Layouts
97	  #       append_view_path "#{Rails.root}/app/views"
98	  #
99	  #       def index
100	  #         render "hello/index"
101	  #       end
102	  #     end
103	  #
104	  # ## Redirection Helpers
105	  #
106	  # To add redirection helpers to your metal controller, do the following:
107	  #
108	  #     class HelloController < ActionController::Metal
109	  #       include ActionController::Redirecting
110	  #       include Rails.application.routes.url_helpers
111	  #
112	  #       def index
113	  #         redirect_to root_url
114	  #       end
115	  #     end
116	  #
117	  # ## Other Helpers
118	  #
119	  # You can refer to the modules included in ActionController::Base to see other
120	  # features you can bring into your metal controller.
121	  class Metal < AbstractController::Base
122	    abstract!
123	
124	    # Returns the last part of the controller's name, underscored, without the
125	    # ending `Controller`. For instance, `PostsController` returns `posts`.
126	    # Namespaces are left out, so `Admin::PostsController` returns `posts` as well.
127	    #
128	    # #### Returns
129	    # *   `string`
130	    def self.controller_name
131	      @controller_name ||= (name.demodulize.delete_suffix("Controller").underscore unless anonymous?)
132	    end
133	
134	    def self.make_response!(request)
135	      ActionDispatch::Response.new.tap do |res|
136	        res.request = request
137	      end
138	    end
139	
140	    def self.action_encoding_template(action) # :nodoc:
141	      false
142	    end

147	          super
148	          subclass.middleware_stack = middleware_stack.dup
149	          subclass.class_eval do
150	            @controller_name = nil
151	          end
152	        end
153	    end
154	
155	    # Delegates to the class's ::controller_name.
156	    def controller_name

158	    end
159	
160	    ##
161	    # :attr_reader: request
162	    #
163	    # The ActionDispatch::Request instance for the current request.

165

169	    # The ActionDispatch::Response instance for the current response.

171

193	
194	    ##

196	    delegate :status, to: "@_response"
197	
198	    ##
199	    # Delegates to ActionDispatch::Response#location
200	    delegate :location, to: "@_response"
201	
202	    ##

211	      @_request = nil
212	      @_response = nil
213	      @_response_body = nil
214	      @_routes = nil
215	      @_params = nil

217	    end

221	    end
222	
223	    def params=(val)
224	      @_params = val
225	    end

228	
229	    # Basic `url_for` that can be overridden for more robust functionality.
230	    def url_for(string)
231	      string
232	    end
233	
234	    def response_body=(body)
235	      if body
236	        body = [body] if body.is_a?(String)
237	        response.body = body
238	        super
239	      else
240	        response.reset_body!
241	      end
242	    end
243	
244	    # Tests if render or redirect has already happened.
245	    def performed?
246	      response_body || response.committed?
247	    end
248	
249	    def dispatch(name, request, response) # :nodoc:
250	      set_request!(request)
251	      set_response!(response)
252	      process(name)
253	      request.commit_flash
254	      to_a
255	    end
256	
257	    def set_response!(response) # :nodoc:
258	      if @_response
259	        _, _, body = @_response
260	        body.close if body.respond_to?(:close)
261	      end
262	
263	      @_response = response
264	    end
265	
266	    # Assign the response and mark it as committed. No further processing will
267	    # occur.
268	    def response=(response)
269	      set_response!(response)
270	
271	      # Force `performed?` to return true:
272	      @_response_body = true
273	    end
274	
275	    def set_request!(request) # :nodoc:
276	      @_request = request
277	      @_request.controller_instance = self
278	    end
279	
280	    def to_a # :nodoc:
281	      response.to_a
282	    end
283	
284	    def reset_session
285	      @_request.reset_session
286	    end

288	    class_attribute :middleware_stack, default: ActionController::MiddlewareStack.new
289	
290	    class << self
291	      # Pushes the given Rack middleware and its arguments to the bottom of the
292	      # middleware stack.
293	      def use(...)
294	        middleware_stack.use(...)
295	      end
296	    end
297	
298	    # The middleware stack used by this controller.
299	    #
300	    # By default uses a variation of ActionDispatch::MiddlewareStack which allows
301	    # for the following syntax:
302	    #
303	    #     class PostsController < ApplicationController
304	    #       use AuthenticationMiddleware, except: [:index, :show]
305	    #     end
306	    #
307	    # Read more about [Rails middleware stack]
308	    # (https://guides.rubyonrails.org/rails_on_rack.html#action-dispatcher-middleware-stack)
309	    # in the guides.
310	    def self.middleware
311	      middleware_stack
312	    end
313	
314	    # Returns a Rack endpoint for the given action name.
315	    def self.action(name)
316	      app = lambda { |env|
317	        req = ActionDispatch::Request.new(env)
318	        res = make_response! req
319	        new.dispatch(name, req, res)
320	      }
321	
322	      if middleware_stack.any?
323	        middleware_stack.build(name, app)
324	      else
325	        app
326	      end
327	    end
328	
329	    # Direct dispatch to the controller. Instantiates the controller, then executes
330	    # the action named `name`.
331	    def self.dispatch(name, req, res)
332	      if middleware_stack.any?
333	        middleware_stack.build(name) { |env| new.dispatch(name, req, res) }.call req.env
334	      else
335	        new.dispatch(name, req, res)
336	      end
337	    end
338	  end
339	end

6	  # This is a class to fix I18n global state. Whenever you provide I18n.locale during a request,
7	  # it will trigger the lookup_context and consequently expire the cache.
8	  class I18nProxy < ::I18n::Config # :nodoc:
9	    attr_reader :original_config, :lookup_context
10	
11	    def initialize(original_config, lookup_context)
12	      original_config = original_config.original_config if original_config.respond_to?(:original_config)
13	      @original_config = original_config
14	      @lookup_context = lookup_context
15	    end
16	
17	    def locale
18	      @original_config.locale
19	    end
20	
21	    def locale=(value)
22	      @lookup_context.locale = value
23	    end
24	  end

39	      old_config, I18n.config = I18n.config, I18nProxy.new(I18n.config, lookup_context)
40	      super
41	    ensure

43	    end

53	        superclass.respond_to?(:view_context_class) &&
54	          supports_path? == superclass.supports_path? &&
55	          _routes.equal?(superclass._routes) &&
56	          _helpers.equal?(superclass._helpers)
57	      end
58	
59	      def build_view_context_class(klass, supports_path, routes, helpers)
60	        if inherit_view_context_class?
61	          return superclass.view_context_class
62	        end
63	
64	        Class.new(klass) do
65	          if routes
66	            include routes.url_helpers(supports_path)
67	            include routes.mounted_helpers
68	          end
69	
70	          if helpers
71	            include helpers
72	          end
73	        end
74	      end
75	
76	      def eager_load!
77	        super
78	        view_context_class
79	        nil
80	      end
81	
82	      def view_context_class
83	        klass = ActionView::LookupContext::DetailsKey.view_context_class
84	
85	        @view_context_class ||= build_view_context_class(klass, supports_path?, _routes, _helpers)
86	
87	        if klass.changed?(@view_context_class)
88	          @view_context_class = build_view_context_class(klass, supports_path?, _routes, _helpers)
89	        end
90	
91	        @view_context_class
92	      end
93	    end
94	
95	    def view_context_class
96	      self.class.view_context_class
97	    end
98	
99	    # An instance of a view class. The default view class is ActionView::Base.
100	    #
101	    # The view class must have the following methods:
102	    #
103	    # * <tt>View.new(lookup_context, assigns, controller)</tt> — Create a new
104	    #   ActionView instance for a controller and we can also pass the arguments.
105	    #
106	    # * <tt>View#render(option)</tt> — Returns String with the rendered template.
107	    #
108	    # Override this method in a module to change the default behavior.
109	    def view_context
110	      view_context_class.new(lookup_context, view_assigns, self)
111	    end
112	
113	    # Returns an object that is able to render templates.
114	    def view_renderer # :nodoc:
115	      # Lifespan: Per controller
116	      @_view_renderer ||= ActionView::Renderer.new(lookup_context)
117	    end
118	
119	    def render_to_body(options = {}, &block)
120	      _process_options(options)
121	      _process_render_template_options(options)
122	      _render_template(options, &block)
123	    end
124	
125	    private
126	      # Find and render a template based on the options given.
127	      def _render_template(options, &block)
128	        variant = options.delete(:variant)
129	        assigns = options.delete(:assigns)
130	        context = view_context
131	
132	        context.assign assigns if assigns
133	        lookup_context.variants = variant if variant
134	
135	        rendered_template = context.in_rendering_context(options) do |renderer|
136	          renderer.render_to_object(context, options, &block)
137	        end
138	
139	        rendered_format = rendered_template.format || lookup_context.formats.first
140	        @_rendered_format = Template::Types[rendered_format]
141	
142	        rendered_template.body
143	      end
144	
145	      # Assign the rendered format to look up context.
146	      def _process_format(format)
147	        super
148	        lookup_context.formats = [format.to_sym] if format.to_sym
149	      end
150	
151	      # Normalize args by converting render "foo" to render action: "foo" and
152	      # render "foo/bar" to render template: "foo/bar".
153	      def _normalize_args(action = nil, options = {})
154	        options = super(action, options)
155	        case action
156	        when NilClass
157	        when Hash
158	          options = action
159	        when String, Symbol
160	          action = action.to_s
161	          key = action.include?(?/) ? :template : :action
162	          options[key] = action
163	        else
164	          if action.respond_to?(:permitted?) && action.permitted?
165	            options = action
166	          elsif action.respond_to?(:render_in)
167	            options[:renderable] = action
168	            options[:locals] = options
169	          else
170	            options[:partial] = action
171	          end
172	        end
173	
174	        options
175	      end
176	
177	      # Normalize options.
178	      def _process_render_template_options(options)
179	        if options[:partial] == true
180	          options[:partial] = action_name
181	        end
182	
183	        if !options.keys.intersect?([:partial, :file, :template])
184	          options[:prefixes] ||= _prefixes
185	        end
186	
187	        options[:template] ||= (options[:action] || action_name).to_s
188	      end
189	  end
190	end

3	require "active_support/core_ext/string/inflections"
4	require "active_support/core_ext/array/conversions"
5	require "active_support/descendants_tracker"
6	require "active_support/dependencies"
7	
8	module Rails
9	  class Application
10	    module Finisher
11	      include Initializable
12	
13	      initializer :add_generator_templates do
14	        ensure_generator_templates_added
15	      end
16	
17	      initializer :setup_main_autoloader do
18	        autoloader = Rails.autoloaders.main
19	
20	        # Normally empty, but if the user already defined some, we won't
21	        # override them. Important if there are custom namespaces associated.
22	        already_configured_dirs = Set.new(autoloader.dirs)
23	
24	        ActiveSupport::Dependencies.autoload_paths.freeze
25	        ActiveSupport::Dependencies.autoload_paths.uniq.each do |path|
26	          # Zeitwerk only accepts existing directories in `push_dir`.
27	          next unless File.directory?(path)
28	          next if already_configured_dirs.member?(path.to_s)
29	
30	          autoloader.push_dir(path)
31	          autoloader.do_not_eager_load(path) unless ActiveSupport::Dependencies.eager_load?(path)
32	        end
33	
34	        if config.reloading_enabled?
35	          autoloader.enable_reloading
36	          ActiveSupport::Dependencies.autoloader = autoloader
37	
38	          autoloader.on_load do |_cpath, value, _abspath|
39	            if value.is_a?(Class) && value.singleton_class < ActiveSupport::DescendantsTracker
40	              ActiveSupport::Dependencies._autoloaded_tracked_classes << value
41	            end
42	          end
43	        end
44	
45	        autoloader.setup
46	      end
47	
48	      # Setup default session store if not already set in config/application.rb
49	      initializer :setup_default_session_store, before: :build_middleware_stack do |app|
50	        unless app.config.session_store?
51	          app_name = app.class.name ? app.railtie_name.chomp("_application") : ""
52	          app.config.session_store :cookie_store, key: "_#{app_name}_session"
53	        end
54	      end
55	
56	      initializer :build_middleware_stack do
57	        build_middleware_stack
58	      end
59	
60	      initializer :define_main_app_helper do |app|
61	        app.routes.define_mounted_helper(:main_app)
62	      end
63	
64	      initializer :add_to_prepare_blocks do |app|
65	        config.to_prepare_blocks.each do |block|
66	          app.reloader.to_prepare(&block)
67	        end
68	      end
69	
70	      # This needs to happen before eager load so it happens
71	      # in exactly the same point regardless of config.eager_load
72	      initializer :run_prepare_callbacks do |app|
73	        app.reloader.prepare!
74	      end
75	
76	      initializer :eager_load! do |app|
77	        if config.eager_load
78	          ActiveSupport.run_load_hooks(:before_eager_load, self)
79	          Zeitwerk::Loader.eager_load_all
80	          Rails.eager_load!
81	          config.eager_load_namespaces.each(&:eager_load!)
82	
83	          if config.reloading_enabled?
84	            app.reloader.after_class_unload do
85	              Rails.autoloaders.main.eager_load
86	            end
87	          end
88	        end
89	      end
90	
91	      # All initialization is done, including eager loading in production
92	      initializer :finisher_hook do
93	        ActiveSupport.run_load_hooks(:after_initialize, self)
94	      end
95	
96	      class MonitorHook # :nodoc:
97	        def initialize(monitor = Monitor.new)
98	          @monitor = monitor
99	        end
100	
101	        def run
102	          @monitor.enter
103	        end
104	
105	        def complete(_state)
106	          @monitor.exit
107	        end
108	      end
109	
110	      module InterlockHook # :nodoc:
111	        def self.run
112	          ActiveSupport::Dependencies.interlock.start_running
113	        end
114	
115	        def self.complete(_state)
116	          ActiveSupport::Dependencies.interlock.done_running
117	        end
118	      end
119	
120	      initializer :configure_executor_for_concurrency do |app|
121	        if config.allow_concurrency == false
122	          # User has explicitly opted out of concurrent request
123	          # handling: presumably their code is not threadsafe
124	
125	          app.executor.register_hook(MonitorHook.new, outer: true)
126	
127	        elsif config.allow_concurrency == :unsafe
128	          # Do nothing, even if we know this is dangerous. This is the
129	          # historical behavior for true.
130	
131	        else
132	          # Default concurrency setting: enabled, but safe
133	
134	          if config.reloading_enabled?
135	            app.executor.register_hook(InterlockHook, outer: true)
136	          end
137	        end
138	      end
139	
140	      initializer :add_internal_routes do |app|
141	        if Rails.env.development?
142	          app.routes.prepend do
143	            get "/rails/info/properties" => "rails/info#properties", internal: true
144	            get "/rails/info/routes"     => "rails/info#routes",     internal: true
145	            get "/rails/info/notes"      => "rails/info#notes",      internal: true
146	            get "/rails/info"            => "rails/info#index",      internal: true
147	            get ".well-known/appspecific/com.chrome.devtools.json" => "rails/devtools#show",      internal: true
148	          end
149	
150	          routes_reloader.run_once_after_load_paths = -> do
151	            app.routes.append do
152	              get "/" => "rails/welcome#index", internal: true
153	            end
154	          end
155	        end
156	      end
157	
158	      # Set routes reload after the finisher hook to ensure routes added in
159	      # the hook are taken into account.
160	      initializer :set_routes_reloader_hook do |app|
161	        reloader = routes_reloader
162	        reloader.eager_load = app.config.eager_load
163	        reloaders << reloader
164	
165	        app.reloader.to_run do
166	          # We configure #execute rather than #execute_if_updated because if
167	          # autoloaded constants are cleared we need to reload routes also in
168	          # case any was used there, as in
169	          #
170	          #   mount MailPreview => 'mail_view'
171	          #
172	          # This means routes are also reloaded if i18n is updated, which
173	          # might not be necessary, but in order to be more precise we need
174	          # some sort of reloaders dependency support, to be added.
175	          require_unload_lock!
176	          reloader.execute
177	          ActiveSupport.run_load_hooks(:after_routes_loaded, self)
178	        end
179	
180	        reloader.execute_unless_loaded if !app.routes.is_a?(Engine::LazyRouteSet) || app.config.eager_load
181	      end
182	
183	      # Set clearing dependencies after the finisher hook to ensure paths
184	      # added in the hook are taken into account.
185	      initializer :set_clear_dependencies_hook, group: :all do |app|
186	        callback = lambda do
187	          # Order matters.
188	          ActiveSupport::DescendantsTracker.clear(ActiveSupport::Dependencies._autoloaded_tracked_classes)
189	          ActiveSupport::Dependencies.clear
190	        end
191	
192	        if config.reloading_enabled?
193	          if config.reload_classes_only_on_change
194	            app.reloader.check = lambda do
195	              app.reloaders.map(&:updated?).any?
196	            end
197	          else
198	            app.reloader.check = lambda { true }
199	          end
200	        else
201	          app.reloader.check = lambda { false }
202	        end
203	
204	        if config.reloading_enabled?
205	          if config.reload_classes_only_on_change
206	            reloader = config.file_watcher.new(*watchable_args, &callback)
207	            reloaders << reloader
208	
209	            # Prepend this callback to have autoloaded constants cleared before
210	            # any other possible reloading, in case they need to autoload fresh
211	            # constants.
212	            app.reloader.to_run(prepend: true) do
213	              # In addition to changes detected by the file watcher, if routes
214	              # or i18n have been updated we also need to clear constants,
215	              # that's why we run #execute rather than #execute_if_updated, this
216	              # callback has to clear autoloaded constants after any update.
217	              class_unload! do
218	                reloader.execute
219	              end
220	            end
221	          else
222	            app.reloader.to_complete do
223	              class_unload!(&callback)
224	            end
225	          end
226	        else
227	          ActiveSupport::DescendantsTracker.disable_clear!
228	        end
229	      end
230	
231	      initializer :enable_yjit do
232	        if config.yjit && defined?(RubyVM::YJIT.enable)
233	          options = config.yjit.is_a?(Hash) ? config.yjit : {}
234	          RubyVM::YJIT.enable(**options)
235	        end
236	      end
237	    end
238	  end
239	end

1	# frozen_string_literal: true
2	
3	module ActionView
4	  # = Action View \Renderer
5	  #
6	  # This is the main entry point for rendering. It basically delegates
7	  # to other objects like TemplateRenderer and PartialRenderer which
8	  # actually renders the template.
9	  #
10	  # The Renderer will parse the options from the +render+ or +render_body+
11	  # method and render a partial or a template based on the options. The
12	  # +TemplateRenderer+ and +PartialRenderer+ objects are wrappers which do all
13	  # the setup and logic necessary to render a view and a new object is created
14	  # each time +render+ is called.
15	  class Renderer
16	    attr_accessor :lookup_context
17	
18	    def initialize(lookup_context)
19	      @lookup_context = lookup_context
20	    end
21	
22	    # Main render entry point shared by Action View and Action Controller.
23	    def render(context, options, &block)
24	      render_to_object(context, options, &block).body
25	    end
26	
27	    def render_to_object(context, options, &block) # :nodoc:
28	      if options.key?(:partial)
29	        render_partial_to_object(context, options)
30	      else
31	        render_template_to_object(context, options, &block)
32	      end
33	    end
34	
35	    # Render but returns a valid Rack body. If fibers are defined, we return
36	    # a streaming body that renders the template piece by piece.
37	    #
38	    # Note that partials are not supported to be rendered with streaming,
39	    # so in such cases, we just wrap them in an array.
40	    def render_body(context, options)
41	      if options.key?(:partial)
42	        [render_partial(context, options)]
43	      else
44	        StreamingTemplateRenderer.new(@lookup_context).render(context, options)
45	      end
46	    end
47	
48	    def render_partial(context, options, &block) # :nodoc:
49	      render_partial_to_object(context, options, &block).body
50	    end
51	
52	    def cache_hits # :nodoc:
53	      @cache_hits ||= {}
54	    end
55	
56	    private
57	      def render_template_to_object(context, options, &block)
58	        TemplateRenderer.new(@lookup_context).render(context, options, &block)
59	      end
60	
61	      def render_partial_to_object(context, options, &block)
62	        partial = options[:partial]
63	        if String === partial
64	          collection = collection_from_options(options)
65	
66	          if collection
67	            # Collection + Partial
68	            renderer = CollectionRenderer.new(@lookup_context, options)
69	            renderer.render_collection_with_partial(collection, partial, context, block)
70	          else
71	            if options.key?(:object)
72	              # Object + Partial
73	              renderer = ObjectRenderer.new(@lookup_context, options)
74	              renderer.render_object_with_partial(options[:object], partial, context, block)
75	            else
76	              # Partial
77	              renderer = PartialRenderer.new(@lookup_context, options)
78	              renderer.render(partial, context, block)
79	            end
80	          end
81	        else
82	          collection = collection_from_object(partial) || collection_from_options(options)
83	
84	          if collection
85	            # Collection + Derived Partial
86	            renderer = CollectionRenderer.new(@lookup_context, options)
87	            renderer.render_collection_derive_partial(collection, context, block)
88	          else
89	            # Object + Derived Partial
90	            renderer = ObjectRenderer.new(@lookup_context, options)
91	            renderer.render_object_derive_partial(partial, context, block)
92	          end
93	        end
94	      end
95	
96	      def collection_from_options(options)
97	        if options.key?(:collection)
98	          collection = options[:collection]
99	          collection || []
100	        end
101	      end
102	
103	      def collection_from_object(object)
104	        object if object.respond_to?(:to_ary)
105	      end
106	  end
107	end
108

1	# frozen_string_literal: true
2	
3	module ActionView
4	  class TemplateRenderer < AbstractRenderer # :nodoc:
5	    def render(context, options, &block)
6	      @details = extract_details(options)
7	      template = determine_template(options, &block)
8	
9	      prepend_formats(template.format)
10	
11	      render_template(context, template, options[:layout], options[:locals] || {})
12	    end
13	
14	    private
15	      # Determine the template to be rendered using the given options.
16	      def determine_template(options, &block)
17	        keys = options.has_key?(:locals) ? options[:locals].keys : []
18	
19	        if options.key?(:body)
20	          Template::Text.new(options[:body])
21	        elsif options.key?(:plain)
22	          Template::Text.new(options[:plain])
23	        elsif options.key?(:html)
24	          Template::HTML.new(options[:html], formats.first)
25	        elsif options.key?(:file)
26	          if File.exist?(options[:file])
27	            Template::RawFile.new(options[:file])
28	          else
29	            if Pathname.new(options[:file]).absolute?
30	              raise ArgumentError, "File #{options[:file]} does not exist"
31	            else
32	              raise ArgumentError, "`render file:` should be given the absolute path to a file. '#{options[:file]}' was given instead"
33	            end
34	          end
35	        elsif options.key?(:inline)
36	          handler = Template.handler_for_extension(options[:type] || "erb")
37	          format = if handler.respond_to?(:default_format)
38	            handler.default_format
39	          else
40	            @lookup_context.formats.first
41	          end
42	          Template::Inline.new(options[:inline], "inline template", handler, locals: keys, format: format)
43	        elsif options.key?(:renderable)
44	          Template::Renderable.new(options[:renderable], &block)
45	        elsif options.key?(:template)
46	          if options[:template].respond_to?(:render)
47	            options[:template]
48	          else
49	            @lookup_context.find_template(options[:template], options[:prefixes], false, keys, @details)
50	          end
51	        else
52	          raise ArgumentError, "You invoked render but did not give any of :body, :file, :html, :inline, :partial, :plain, :renderable, or :template option."
53	        end
54	      end
55	
56	      # Renders the given template. A string representing the layout can be
57	      # supplied as well.
58	      def render_template(view, template, layout_name, locals)
59	        render_with_layout(view, template, layout_name, locals) do |layout|
60	          ActiveSupport::Notifications.instrument(
61	            "render_template.action_view",
62	            identifier: template.identifier,
63	            layout: layout && layout.virtual_path,
64	            locals: locals
65	          ) do
66	            template.render(view, locals) { |*name| view._layout_for(*name) }
67	          end
68	        end
69	      end
70	
71	      def render_with_layout(view, template, path, locals)
72	        layout  = path && find_layout(path, locals.keys, [formats.first])
73	
74	        body = if layout
75	          ActiveSupport::Notifications.instrument("render_layout.action_view", identifier: layout.identifier) do
76	            view.view_flow.set(:layout, yield(layout))
77	            layout.render(view, locals) { |*name| view._layout_for(*name) }
78	          end
79	        else
80	          yield
81	        end
82	        build_rendered_template(body, template)
83	      end
84	
85	      # This is the method which actually finds the layout using details in the lookup
86	      # context object. If no layout is found, it checks if at least a layout with
87	      # the given name exists across all details before raising the error.
88	      def find_layout(layout, keys, formats)
89	        resolve_layout(layout, keys, formats)
90	      end
91	
92	      def resolve_layout(layout, keys, formats)
93	        details = @details.dup
94	        details[:formats] = formats
95	
96	        case layout
97	        when String
98	          begin
99	            if layout.start_with?("/")
100	              raise ArgumentError, "Rendering layouts from an absolute path is not supported."
101	            else
102	              @lookup_context.find_template(layout, nil, false, keys, details)
103	            end
104	          rescue ActionView::MissingTemplate
105	            all_details = @details.merge(formats: @lookup_context.default_formats)
106	            raise unless template_exists?(layout, nil, false, keys, **all_details)
107	          end
108	        when Proc
109	          resolve_layout(layout.call(@lookup_context, formats, keys), keys, formats)
110	        else
111	          layout
112	        end
113	      end
114	  end
115	end

1	# frozen_string_literal: true
2	
3	require "delegate"
4	
5	module ActionView
6	  # = Action View \Template
7	  class Template
8	    extend ActiveSupport::Autoload
9	
10	    STRICT_LOCALS_REGEX = /\#\s+locals:\s+\((.*?)\)(?=\s*-?%>|\s*$)/m
11	
12	    # === Encodings in ActionView::Template
13	    #
14	    # ActionView::Template is one of a few sources of potential
15	    # encoding issues in \Rails. This is because the source for
16	    # templates are usually read from disk, and Ruby (like most
17	    # encoding-aware programming languages) assumes that the
18	    # String retrieved through File IO is encoded in the
19	    # <tt>default_external</tt> encoding. In \Rails, the default
20	    # <tt>default_external</tt> encoding is UTF-8.
21	    #
22	    # As a result, if a user saves their template as ISO-8859-1
23	    # (for instance, using a non-Unicode-aware text editor),
24	    # and uses characters outside of the ASCII range, their
25	    # users will see diamonds with question marks in them in
26	    # the browser.
27	    #
28	    # For the rest of this documentation, when we say "UTF-8",
29	    # we mean "UTF-8 or whatever the default_internal encoding
30	    # is set to". By default, it will be UTF-8.
31	    #
32	    # To mitigate this problem, we use a few strategies:
33	    # 1. If the source is not valid UTF-8, we raise an exception
34	    #    when the template is compiled to alert the user
35	    #    to the problem.
36	    # 2. The user can specify the encoding using Ruby-style
37	    #    encoding comments in any template engine. If such
38	    #    a comment is supplied, \Rails will apply that encoding
39	    #    to the resulting compiled source returned by the
40	    #    template handler.
41	    # 3. In all cases, we transcode the resulting String to
42	    #    the UTF-8.
43	    #
44	    # This means that other parts of \Rails can always assume
45	    # that templates are encoded in UTF-8, even if the original
46	    # source of the template was not UTF-8.
47	    #
48	    # From a user's perspective, the easiest thing to do is
49	    # to save your templates as UTF-8. If you do this, you
50	    # do not need to do anything else for things to "just work".
51	    #
52	    # === Instructions for template handlers
53	    #
54	    # The easiest thing for you to do is to simply ignore
55	    # encodings. \Rails will hand you the template source
56	    # as the default_internal (generally UTF-8), raising
57	    # an exception for the user before sending the template
58	    # to you if it could not determine the original encoding.
59	    #

In the Rails monorepo at /home/bench/repos/rails, I need to map the full default middleware stack and the key middleware that transforms a Rack env on the way in and out. Investigate:

1. `railties/lib/rails/application/default_middleware_stack.rb` — read the full file and list every middleware added in order with line numbers.
2. Key middleware implementations and their `call` methods:
   - `ActionDispatch::Executor` — actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/middleware/executor.rb
   - `ActionDispatch::RequestId` — actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/middleware/request_id.rb  
   - `ActionDispatch::Static` — actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/middleware/static.rb
   - `ActionDispatch::Session::CookieStore` — how session is added to request
   - `ActionDispatch::Flash` — actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/middleware/flash.rb
   - `ActionDispatch::Router` — how it wraps RouteSet
3. `ActionDispatch::Request` — how env is wrapped into a Request object. File: actionpack/lib/action_dispatch/http/request.rb — find `initialize` and key methods

content.content:
1	# frozen_string_literal: true
2	
3	module Rails
4	  class Application
5	    class DefaultMiddlewareStack
6	      attr_reader :config, :paths, :app
7	
8	      def initialize(app, config, paths)
9	        @app = app
10	        @config = config
11	        @paths = paths
12	      end
13	
14	      def build_stack
15	        ActionDispatch::MiddlewareStack.new do |middleware|
16	          unless Array(config.hosts).empty?
17	            middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::HostAuthorization, config.hosts, **config.host_authorization
18	          end
19	
20	          if config.assume_ssl
21	            middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::AssumeSSL
22	          end
23	
24	          if config.force_ssl
25	            middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::SSL, **config.ssl_options,
26	              ssl_default_redirect_status: config.action_dispatch.ssl_default_redirect_status
27	          end
28	
29	          if config.action_dispatch.x_sendfile_header
30	            middleware.use ::Rack::Sendfile, config.action_dispatch.x_sendfile_header
31	          end
32	
33	          if config.public_file_server.enabled
34	            headers = config.public_file_server.headers || {}
35	
36	            middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::Static, paths["public"].first, index: config.public_file_server.index_name, headers: headers
37	          end
38	
39	          if rack_cache = load_rack_cache
40	            require "action_dispatch/http/rack_cache"
41	            middleware.use ::Rack::Cache, rack_cache
42	          end
43	
44	          if config.allow_concurrency == false
45	            # User has explicitly opted out of concurrent request
46	            # handling: presumably their code is not threadsafe
47	
48	            middleware.use ::Rack::Lock
49	          end
50	
51	          middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::Executor, app.executor
52	
53	          middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::ServerTiming if config.server_timing
54	          middleware.use ::Rack::Runtime
55	          middleware.use ::Rack::MethodOverride unless config.api_only
56	          middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::RequestId, header: config.action_dispatch.request_id_header
57	          middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::RemoteIp, config.action_dispatch.ip_spoofing_check, config.action_dispatch.trusted_proxies
58	
59	          if path = config.silence_healthcheck_path
60	            middleware.use ::Rails::Rack::SilenceRequest, path: path
61	          end
62	
63	          middleware.use ::Rails::Rack::Logger, config.log_tags
64	          middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::ShowExceptions, show_exceptions_app
65	          middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::DebugExceptions, app, config.debug_exception_response_format
66	
67	          if config.consider_all_requests_local
68	            middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::ActionableExceptions
69	          end
70	
71	          if config.reloading_enabled?
72	            middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::Reloader, app.reloader
73	          end
74	
75	          middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::Callbacks
76	          middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::Cookies unless config.api_only
77	
78	          if !config.api_only && config.session_store
79	            if config.force_ssl && config.ssl_options.fetch(:secure_cookies, true) && !config.session_options.key?(:secure)
80	              config.session_options[:secure] = true
81	            end
82	            middleware.use config.session_store, config.session_options
83	          end
84	
85	          unless config.api_only
86	            middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::Flash
87	            middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::ContentSecurityPolicy::Middleware
88	            middleware.use ::ActionDispatch::PermissionsPolicy::Middleware if config.permissions_policy
89	          end
90	
91	          middleware.use ::Rack::Head
92	          middleware.use ::Rack::ConditionalGet
93	          middleware.use ::Rack::ETag, "no-cache"
94	
95	          middleware.use ::Rack::TempfileReaper unless config.api_only
96	
97	          if config.respond_to?(:active_record)
98	            if selector_options = config.active_record.database_selector
99	              resolver = config.active_record.database_resolver
100	              context = config.active_record.database_resolver_context
101	
102	              middleware.use ::ActiveRecord::Middleware::DatabaseSelector, resolver, context, selector_options
103	            end
104	
105	            if shard_resolver = config.active_record.shard_resolver
106	              options = config.active_record.shard_selector || {}
107	
108	              middleware.use ::ActiveRecord::Middleware::ShardSelector, shard_resolver, options
109	            end
110	          end
111	        end
112	      end
113	
114	      private
115	        def load_rack_cache
116	          rack_cache = config.action_dispatch.rack_cache
117	          return unless rack_cache
118	
119	          begin
120	            require "rack/cache"
121	          rescue LoadError => error
122	            error.message << " Be sure to add rack-cache to your Gemfile"
123	            raise
124	          end
125	
126	          if rack_cache == true
127	            {
128	              metastore: "rails:/",
129	              entitystore: "rails:/",
130	              verbose: false
131	            }
132	          else
133	            rack_cache
134	          end
135	        end
136	
137	        def show_exceptions_app
138	          config.exceptions_app || ActionDispatch::PublicExceptions.new(Rails.public_path)
139	        end
140	    end
141	  end
142	end
143

7	module ActionDispatch
8	  class Executor
9	    def initialize(app, executor)
10	      @app, @executor = app, executor
11	    end
12	
13	    def call(env)
14	      state = @executor.run!(reset: true)
15	
16	      completed = false
17	      finalize = -> {
18	        next if completed
19	        completed = true
20	        state.complete!
21	      }
22	
23	      if response_finished = env["rack.response_finished"]
24	        response_finished << proc { finalize.call }
25	      end
26	
27	      begin
28	        response = @app.call(env)
29	
30	        if env["action_dispatch.report_exception"]
31	          error = env["action_dispatch.exception"]
32	          @executor.error_reporter.report(error, handled: false, source: "application.action_dispatch")
33	        end
34	
35	        if hijacked?(env, response)
36	          # `close` and `rack.response_finished` may never fire on hijack.
37	          # Release eagerly; the request's autoloaded code is resolved by now.
38	          finalize.call
39	        elsif !response_finished
40	          response << ::Rack::BodyProxy.new(response.pop) { finalize.call }
41	        end
42	        returned = true
43	        response
44	      rescue Exception => error
45	        request = ActionDispatch::Request.new env
46	        backtrace_cleaner = request.get_header("action_dispatch.backtrace_cleaner")
47	        wrapper = ExceptionWrapper.new(backtrace_cleaner, error)
48	        @executor.error_reporter.report(wrapper.unwrapped_exception, handled: false, source: "application.action_dispatch")
49	        raise
50	      ensure
51	        if !returned && !response_finished
52	          finalize.call
53	        end
54	      end
55	    end
56	
57	    private
58	      def hijacked?(env, response)
59	        return false unless response
60	
61	        env["rack.hijack_io"] || response.first == 101
62	      end
63	  end
64	end

5	require "securerandom"
6	require "active_support/core_ext/string/access"
7	
8	module ActionDispatch
9	  # # Action Dispatch RequestId
10	  #
11	  # Makes a unique request id available to the `action_dispatch.request_id` env
12	  # variable (which is then accessible through ActionDispatch::Request#request_id
13	  # or the alias ActionDispatch::Request#uuid) and sends the same id to the client
14	  # via the `X-Request-Id` header.
15	  #
16	  # The unique request id is either based on the `X-Request-Id` header in the
17	  # request, which would typically be generated by a firewall, load balancer, or
18	  # the web server, or, if this header is not available, a random uuid. If the
19	  # header is accepted from the outside world, we sanitize it to a max of 255
20	  # chars and alphanumeric and dashes only.
21	  #
22	  # The unique request id can be used to trace a request end-to-end and would
23	  # typically end up being part of log files from multiple pieces of the stack.
24	  class RequestId
25	    def initialize(app, header:)
26	      @app = app
27	      @header = header
28	      @env_header = "HTTP_#{header.upcase.tr("-", "_")}"
29	    end
30	
31	    def call(env)
32	      req = ActionDispatch::Request.new env
33	      req.request_id = make_request_id(req.get_header(@env_header))
34	      @app.call(env).tap { |_status, headers, _body| headers[@header] = req.request_id }
35	    end
36	
37	    private
38	      def make_request_id(request_id)
39	        if request_id.presence
40	          request_id.gsub(/[^\w\-@]/, "").first(255)
41	        else
42	          internal_request_id
43	        end
44	      end
45	
46	      def internal_request_id
47	        SecureRandom.uuid
48	      end
49	  end
50	end

3	require "concurrent/map"
4	require "active_support/core_ext/module/attribute_accessors"
5	require "action_view/template/resolver"
6	
7	module ActionView
8	  # = Action View Lookup Context
9	  #
10	  # <tt>LookupContext</tt> is the object responsible for holding all information
11	  # required for looking up templates, i.e. view paths and details.
12	  # <tt>LookupContext</tt> is also responsible for generating a key, given to
13	  # view paths, used in the resolver cache lookup. Since this key is generated
14	  # only once during the request, it speeds up all cache accesses.
15	  class LookupContext # :nodoc:
16	    attr_accessor :prefixes
17	
18	    singleton_class.attr_accessor :default_procs
19	    self.default_procs = {}.freeze
20	
21	    def self.registered_details
22	      self.default_procs.keys
23	    end
24	
25	    def self.register_detail(name, &block)
26	      self.default_procs = self.default_procs.merge(name => block).freeze
27	
28	      Accessors.define_method(:"default_#{name}", &block)
29	      Accessors.module_eval <<-METHOD, __FILE__, __LINE__ + 1
30	        def #{name}
31	          @details[:#{name}] || []
32	        end
33	
34	        def #{name}=(value)
35	          value = value.present? ? Array(value) : default_#{name}
36	          _set_detail(:#{name}, value) if value != @details[:#{name}]
37	        end
38	      METHOD
39	    end
40	
41	    # Holds accessors for the registered details.
42	    module Accessors # :nodoc:
43	    end
44	
45	    register_detail(:locale) do
46	      locales = [I18n.locale]
47	      locales.concat(I18n.fallbacks[I18n.locale]) if I18n.respond_to? :fallbacks
48	      locales << I18n.default_locale
49	      locales.uniq!
50	      locales
51	    end
52	    register_detail(:formats) { ActionView::Base.default_formats || [:html, :text, :js, :css,  :xml, :json] }
53	    register_detail(:variants) { [] }
54	    register_detail(:handlers) { Template::Handlers.extensions }
55	
56	    class DetailsKey # :nodoc:
57	      alias :eql? :equal?
58	
59	      @details_keys = Concurrent::Map.new
60	      @digest_cache = Concurrent::Map.new
61	      @view_context_mutex = Mutex.new
62	
63	      def self.digest_cache(details)
64	        @digest_cache[details_cache_key(details)] ||= Concurrent::Map.new
65	      end
66	
67	      def self.details_cache_key(details)
68	        @details_keys.fetch(details) do
69	          if formats = details[:formats]
70	            if normalized = Template.normalized_formats(formats)
71	              details = details.dup
72	              details[:formats] = normalized
73	            end
74	          end
75	          @details_keys[details] ||= TemplateDetails::Requested.new(**details)
76	        end
77	      end
78	
79	      def self.clear
80	        ActionView::PathRegistry.all_resolvers.each do |resolver|
81	          resolver.clear_cache
82	        end
83	        @view_context_class = nil
84	        @details_keys.clear
85	        @digest_cache.clear
86	      end
87	
88	      def self.digest_caches
89	        @digest_cache.values
90	      end
91	
92	      def self.view_context_class
93	        @view_context_mutex.synchronize do
94	          @view_context_class ||= ActionView::Base.with_empty_template_cache
95	        end
96	      end
97	    end
98	
99	    # Add caching behavior on top of Details.
100	    module DetailsCache
101	      attr_accessor :cache
102	
103	      # Calculate the details key. Remove the handlers from calculation to improve performance
104	      # since the user cannot modify it explicitly.
105	      def details_key # :nodoc:
106	        @details_key ||= DetailsKey.details_cache_key(@details) if @cache
107	      end
108	
109	      # Temporary skip passing the details_key forward.
110	      def disable_cache
111	        old_value, @cache = @cache, false
112	        yield
113	      ensure
114	        @cache = old_value
115	      end
116	
117	    private
118	      def _set_detail(key, value) # :doc:
119	        @details = @details.dup if @digest_cache || @details_key
120	        @digest_cache = nil
121	        @details_key = nil
122	        @details[key] = value
123	      end
124	    end
125	
126	    # Helpers related to template lookup using the lookup context information.
127	    module ViewPaths
128	      attr_reader :view_paths, :html_fallback_for_js
129	
130	      def find(name, prefixes = [], partial = false, keys = [], options = {})
131	        name, prefixes = normalize_name(name, prefixes)
132	        details, details_key = detail_args_for(options)
133	        @view_paths.find(name, prefixes, partial, details, details_key, keys)
134	      end
135	      alias :find_template :find
136	
137	      def find_all(name, prefixes = [], partial = false, keys = [], options = {})
138	        name, prefixes = normalize_name(name, prefixes)
139	        details, details_key = detail_args_for(options)
140	        @view_paths.find_all(name, prefixes, partial, details, details_key, keys)
141	      end
142	
143	      def exists?(name, prefixes = [], partial = false, keys = [], **options)
144	        name, prefixes = normalize_name(name, prefixes)
145	        details, details_key = detail_args_for(options)
146	        @view_paths.exists?(name, prefixes, partial, details, details_key, keys)
147	      end
148	      alias :template_exists? :exists?
149	
150	      def any?(name, prefixes = [], partial = false)
